text:
INPATIENT ONCOLOGY CONSULT NOTE
Hospital Day 4 — Immune-Related Adverse Event Management

---

ATTENDING: [Attending Oncologist]
CONSULTING SERVICE: Hematology/Oncology
REQUESTING SERVICE: Hospital Medicine / Gastroenterology
REASON FOR CONSULT: Grade 3 immune-mediated colitis in the setting of Yervoy/nivo therapy; guidance on immunosuppression, oncologic management, and disposition

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Renata Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 56-year-old woman with Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma currently on cycle 3 of ipi (1 mg/kg) plus nivo (3 mg/kg) combination immunotherapy, admitted 4 days ago with a 4-day history of profuse watery and bloody diarrhea (up to 10-12 episodes per day at nadir), severe periumbilical and lower abdominal cramping, and low-grade fever to 38.6°C. She is now on hospital day 4 of IV corticosteroid therapy and is showing meaningful clinical improvement.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo's oncologic history began approximately 18 months ago when she was diagnosed at age 55 with Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition, M1c) cutaneous malignant melanoma, superficial spreading type, arising from the left posterior shoulder. The primary lesion measured 4.2 mm Breslow thickness, Clark level V, with ulceration, 4 mitoses/mm2, and lymphovascular invasion present. At diagnosis she had bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy, multiple subcutaneous nodules (right flank and left axilla), and four hepatic metastases (largest 3.1 cm in the right lobe), confirmed on whole-body PET-CT. Baseline LDH was elevated at 387 U/L (approximately 1.5x ULN). Molecular profiling of a right axillary lymph node core biopsy demonstrated a BRAF p.V600E mutation (VAF 48%), CDKN2A homozygous deletion, PTEN p.R130Q mutation, TP53 p.R248W, MYC amplification, and a tumor mutational burden of 42 mutations/Mb (high). PD-L1 TPS was 45%.

Given rapid disease kinetics and the need for prompt tumor burden reduction, she was initiated on first-line dabra 150 mg BID plus tram 2 mg daily. She achieved a marked partial response at 8-week restaging (approximately 62% reduction in target lesion sum), with LDH normalizing and her right flank subcutaneous nodule becoming non-palpable. Her course on BRAFi/MEKi was complicated by two episodes of dabra-related pyrexia — the first (grade 1-2, temperature 38.4°C) managed with brief drug holiday and acetaminophen, and the second (grade 2, temperature 39.1°C with rigors) necessitating an emergency department visit, 5-day drug hold, and a first dose reduction of dabra to 100 mg BID. She also had grade 1 maculopapular rash and grade 1 hand-foot syndrome, both manageable with topical steroids and supportive care.

After approximately 8 months of Tafinlar/tram, restaging CT demonstrated clear radiographic progression: growth of the right axillary lymph node (from 0.5 cm to 1.6 cm), a new left axillary lymph node, interval enlargement of known hepatic metastases, a new right hepatic lobe lesion, and two new right lower lobe pulmonary nodules. LDH rose to 512 U/L. Clinically, she reported new right upper quadrant discomfort, mild exertional dyspnea, and 6 lb weight loss over 4 weeks. Acquired resistance to BRAFi/MEKi combination therapy was concluded.

Given her high TMB (42 mut/Mb), PD-L1 TPS 45%, and overall fitness (ECOG PS 1), she was transitioned to second-line combination immunotherapy with Yervoy 1 mg/kg IV plus nivo 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks (CheckMate-067 low-dose ipi schema). She received cycles 1 and 2 without significant adverse events. At approximately 6 weeks into treatment (cycle 3 administration), she developed a progressive increase in stool frequency over 3-4 days. By the time of ED presentation, she was having 10-12 watery and bloody bowel movements per day with severe cramping and fevers. She was afebrile to 38.6°C in the ED. Labs were notable for hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL (mild anemia), CRP 94 mg/L (significantly elevated), and markedly elevated fecal calprotectin. Infectious workup was comprehensively negative: C. difficile PCR negative, stool cultures negative, ova and parasites negative, and CMV colitis not identified on biopsy. Flexible sigmoidoscopy demonstrated diffuse moderate-to-severe colitis from the rectum extending to the descending colon, endoscopically and histologically consistent with immune-related colitis. Both Yervoy and Opdivo were held upon admission.

She was started on high-dose IV methylprednisolone at 2 mg/kg/day (approximately 130 mg IV daily). Gastroenterology was consulted and remains co-managing. She is now on hospital day 4, and this consult note reflects her current status and forward management plan.

Today, she reports that bowel movements have decreased substantially to approximately 4-5 per day, stools are now formed or semi-formed without gross blood. Abdominal cramping is significantly improved, rating the pain as 2/10 (from 7-8/10 on admission). She is tolerating a low-residue oral diet. She denies fever over the past 36 hours. She denies nausea or vomiting. No new skin rashes, eye symptoms, chest pain, or shortness of breath. No signs or symptoms to suggest other concurrent immune-mediated organ toxicities.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Fevers resolved for >36 hours. Fatigue improving but still present. No chills or rigors currently.
Gastrointestinal: Diarrhea markedly improved (now 4-5 formed/semi-formed BMs/day, no blood). Cramping improved (2/10). Tolerating oral low-residue diet. No nausea or vomiting.
Endocrine: No symptoms of adrenal insufficiency (no lightheadedness, hypotension, or salt craving). No polyuria, polydipsia. Hypothyroidism: patient notes her baseline hypothyroidism (on levothyroxine pre-dating immunotherapy) — no new thyroid symptoms.
Muscle/joint: No arthralgias, myalgias, or muscle weakness.
Skin: No new rashes, blistering, or skin lesions. No oral ulcers.
Ocular: No visual changes, eye redness, or pain.
Respiratory: No new cough, dyspnea, or chest pain.
Neurological: No headache, confusion, limb weakness, or sensory changes.
Genitourinary: Normal urine output. No hematuria or dysuria.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY

1. Stage IV (M1c) BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous malignant melanoma, superficial spreading type, left posterior shoulder — as above
   - Primary: Breslow 4.2 mm, Clark level V, ulcerated, 4 mitoses/mm2, LVI present
   - Metastatic sites at diagnosis: bilateral axillary LNs, right flank and left axillary subcutaneous nodules, four hepatic lesions
   - BRAF p.V600E (VAF 48%), CDKN2A homozygous deletion, PTEN p.R130Q, TP53 p.R248W; TMB 42 mut/Mb; PD-L1 TPS 45%
   - Prior therapies: Tafinlar + tram (1L, ~8 months, partial response then progression); ipi + nivo (2L, cycles 1-3, held due to current toxicity)
2. Hypothyroidism — on levothyroxine (pre-existing, diagnosed prior to cancer diagnosis)
3. Former smoker — 10 pack-year history, quit approximately 20 years prior to current admission

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY

1. Wide local excision of primary left posterior shoulder melanoma with sentinel lymph node evaluation (performed at time of diagnosis workup)

FAMILY HISTORY

Not formally documented in current chart. Reportedly no known family history of melanoma or other skin cancers. No known family history of autoimmune or inflammatory bowel disease.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 56-year-old landscape architect who resides in the local area. She has a significant history of occupational and recreational sun exposure, consistent with her profession. She is of mixed Afro-Caribbean and Eastern European descent. Former tobacco smoker (10 pack-years, cessation approximately 20 years ago). No current alcohol or recreational substance use reported. She is married with adult children. She was working full-time until her admission. Excellent social support system and engaged in her oncologic care.

ALLERGIES

No known drug allergies. No known food allergies.

MEDICATIONS (on admission; current inpatient medications)

1. Methylprednisolone sodium succinate 130 mg IV every 24 hours (2 mg/kg/day based on weight ~65 kg) — immune-mediated colitis
2. Levothyroxine 75 mcg orally once daily (dose per outpatient regimen; note: dose was not yet increased at time of admission)
3. Omeprazole 40 mg orally once daily — GI prophylaxis during high-dose steroid therapy
4. Ondansetron 4 mg IV/PO every 8 hours as needed — nausea
5. Acetaminophen 650 mg orally every 6 hours as needed — pain/discomfort
6. Heparin 5000 units subcutaneously every 8 hours — VTE prophylaxis
7. IV normal saline with potassium chloride 20 mEq/L at 75 mL/hr — hydration and electrolyte repletion (IV fluids being weaned as oral intake improves)

Held medications:
- ipi — held pending colitis resolution and treatment decision
- Opdivo — held pending colitis resolution; rechallenge to be determined
- dabra — previously discontinued at time of confirmed progression
- Mekinist — previously discontinued at time of confirmed progression

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vitals (today): Temperature 37.2°C (afebrile), Heart rate 84 bpm (regular), Blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, Respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, SpO2 98% on room air, Weight 63.8 kg (down ~2 kg from pre-admission weight).

General: Alert, oriented, appears fatigued but more comfortable than on admission. No acute distress. Well-nourished.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Anicteric sclerae. No oral mucositis or ulcerations. No conjunctival injection.

Skin: No erythematous rash. Well-healed surgical scar over the left posterior shoulder from prior wide local excision. No palpable subcutaneous nodules. No skin breakdown at IV sites.

Lymph nodes: No palpable axillary lymphadenopathy appreciated bilaterally today (previously documented bilateral axillary adenopathy, now resolved on prior imaging during immunotherapy).

Chest/Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rales, or rhonchi.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No lower extremity edema (peripheral edema previously noted on BRAFi — resolved after drug discontinuation).

Abdomen: Soft. Mild diffuse tenderness to deep palpation throughout the lower abdomen and left lower quadrant; no guarding; no rebound tenderness. Normoactive bowel sounds. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciated on palpation. No peritoneal signs.

Neurological: Alert and oriented x4. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal motor or sensory deficits. Normal gait.

Musculoskeletal: No joint swelling or tenderness. No muscle weakness.

Rectal (per gastroenterology exam on admission): Perianal area without ulceration; sigmoidoscopy findings as documented above.

---

LABORATORY AND DIAGNOSTIC RESULTS

Most Recent Labs (Hospital Day 4):

CBC:
- WBC 11.2 x10^9/L (mild leukocytosis, likely steroid-related)
- Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL (improving from 10.2 g/dL on admission; consistent with iron deficiency and chronic inflammation in setting of GI blood loss)
- Platelets 318 x10^9/L
- Differential: Neutrophils 78% (neutrophilia expected on steroids), Lymphocytes 12%, Monocytes 7%, Eosinophils 1%

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Na 137 mEq/L, K 3.6 mEq/L (low-normal, ongoing repletion), Cl 102 mEq/L, Bicarb 26 mEq/L
- BUN 14 mg/dL, Creatinine 0.82 mg/dL (baseline renal function normal)
- Glucose 164 mg/dL (steroid-induced hyperglycemia; endocrinology/nursing aware)
- Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, Direct bilirubin 0.2 mg/dL
- ALT 44 U/L, AST 38 U/L (mild, improved from pre-admission; no hepatitis pattern)
- Alkaline phosphatase 88 U/L
- Albumin 3.4 g/dL (mildly low, consistent with acute phase response and reduced oral intake)

Inflammatory Markers:
- CRP 24 mg/L (significantly improved from 94 mg/L on admission; trending down nicely)
- ESR 48 mm/hr

Immune-Endocrine Panel:
- TSH: 3.4 mIU/L (within normal range on current levothyroxine dose; re-checking baseline given known risk of immune thyroiditis with checkpoint inhibitors)
- Free T4: 0.9 ng/dL (low-normal)
- Morning cortisol: 14.2 mcg/dL (adequate; no evidence of adrenal insufficiency)
- ACTH stimulation test: not indicated at this time

Stool Studies (admission, all finalized as negative):
- C. difficile PCR: NEGATIVE
- Stool culture: No enteric pathogens isolated
- Ova and parasites: NEGATIVE
- CMV immunostaining on biopsy: NEGATIVE

Fecal Calprotectin (admission): >1800 mcg/g (markedly elevated; repeat not yet available)

LDH: 312 U/L (elevated — this is an important tumor marker to track; elevated at time of immunotherapy initiation [512 U/L] and is now trending down, consistent with tumor response; context: LDH is being closely followed)

Coagulation: PT/INR 1.1, PTT 29 seconds (normal)

Endoscopy (performed on day of admission): Flexible sigmoidoscopy demonstrated diffuse moderate-to-severe colitis from the rectum extending to the descending colon. Biopsies obtained. Pathology consistent with immune-related (checkpoint inhibitor-associated) colitis. No CMV inclusions. No pseudomembranes.

Imaging: CT abdomen/pelvis obtained on admission demonstrated colonic wall thickening of the sigmoid and descending colon consistent with colitis, no evidence of free air or perforation, no toxic megacolon, and no other acute abdominal pathology. Incidentally noted: interval decrease in hepatic lesion size compared to prior imaging, consistent with ongoing tumor response to immunotherapy prior to drug hold — right hepatic lobe lesions approximately 1.4 cm and 0.7 cm (previously 2.3 cm and 1.4 cm prior to immunotherapy).

---

IMAGING SUMMARY (PRIOR, FOR ONCOLOGIC CONTEXT)

Most recent dedicated restaging CT (obtained approximately 10 weeks after initiating ipi/nivo, concurrent with this admission CT): As noted above, the hepatic disease appears to be responding to immunotherapy, with interval decrease in the right lobe hepatic lesions (previously 2.3 cm and 1.4 cm at progression on BRAFi/MEKi; now approximately 1.4 cm and 0.7 cm), new hepatic lesion no longer identified, and the pulmonary nodules that were seen at progression now appear smaller (right lower lobe: 0.3 cm and 0.2 cm). The axillary nodes have also decreased (right axilla now 0.9 cm from 1.6 cm; left axilla 0.6 cm from 1.1 cm). LDH trending down from 512 to currently 312 U/L. This is encouraging partial response to combination immunotherapy, occurring in the context of this toxicity.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 56-year-old woman with Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma, previously treated with dabra/tram (first-line, with partial response and subsequent progression at approximately 8 months), now on second-line Yervoy/nivo combination immunotherapy, presenting with grade 3 immune-mediated colitis after cycle 3, now on hospital day 4 with clear clinical improvement on high-dose IV methylprednisolone.

PROBLEM 1: GRADE 3 IMMUNE-MEDIATED (CHECKPOINT INHIBITOR-ASSOCIATED) COLITIS

The diagnosis of grade 3 irAE colitis is well-established in this case. The clinical picture, endoscopic appearance, histopathology (immune-infiltrate colitis pattern without infectious etiology), and negative infectious workup all point firmly to immune checkpoint inhibitor-induced colitis. The severe presentation with 10-12 bloody stools/day (grade 3 per CTCAE v5.0) and CRP of 94 mg/L on admission required immediate drug hold and initiation of high-dose systemic corticosteroids per ASCO and ESMO immune toxicity guidelines.

- Corticosteroids: She is responding very well to IV methylprednisolone 2 mg/kg/day (currently day 4 of treatment). Stool frequency has reduced from 10-12 to 4-5 stools/day, with resolution of gross blood and significant pain improvement. CRP has fallen from 94 to 24 mg/L. This trajectory is reassuring.
  - Plan: Continue IV methylprednisolone for a total of approximately 3-5 days IV, then transition to oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day (approximately 65 mg daily) once stool frequency is ≤3/day with no blood for 24 hours. I anticipate transitioning tomorrow or the day after.
  - Planned oral prednisone taper: Very gradual taper over 6-8 weeks minimum per ASCO immune toxicity guidelines — rapid steroid taper in this setting is associated with colitis flare and relapse. We will taper by approximately 10-20% every 1-2 weeks, guided by symptoms.
  - If she fails to continue improving or worsens over the next 24-48 hours, we should have a low threshold to add infliximab (5 mg/kg IV) or vedolizumab as second-line immunosuppression — I discussed this with gastroenterology and they are in agreement. At this time, her trajectory does not require this escalation.
- GI prophylaxis: Continue omeprazole throughout steroid course. Calcium 500 mg BID and vitamin D 800 IU daily added today to mitigate steroid-induced bone density loss.
- Steroid-induced hyperglycemia: Glucose 164 mg/dL today — nursing to continue glucose monitoring with meals and at bedtime. If consistently >200 mg/dL, will involve endocrinology/diabetes management service for correction dosing.
- Bowel rest: Low-residue diet as tolerated; IV fluids weaning as oral intake improves.
- Colonoscopy: Not required at this time; flexible sigmoidoscopy with biopsy was diagnostic. May consider follow-up colonoscopy after clinical remission to confirm mucosal healing prior to any immunotherapy rechallenge, per gastroenterology's recommendation.

PROBLEM 2: ipi — PERMANENT DISCONTINUATION

Given grade 3 immune-mediated colitis, ipi is permanently discontinued per ASCO guidelines and our institutional protocol. This is a non-negotiable decision. I discussed this clearly with the patient and her family today. Grade 3 colitis on ipi mandates permanent cessation of the anti-CTLA-4 agent; rechallenge carries unacceptable risk of recurrent and potentially life-threatening toxicity including perforation and toxic megacolon.

PROBLEM 3: Opdivo RECHALLENGE — PLAN FOR FUTURE CONSIDERATION

Opdivo is currently held. The question of rechallenge with anti-PD-1 monotherapy after grade 3 colitis is a nuanced clinical decision. In this patient's case, there are several factors strongly favoring eventual Opdivo rechallenge:
- Tumor is demonstrably responding to immunotherapy (ongoing CT response, LDH trending down)
- High TMB (42 mut/Mb) and PD-L1 TPS 45% suggest strong immunotherapy sensitivity
- The mechanism of anti-PD-1 and anti-CTLA-4 toxicities differ somewhat, and published data suggest anti-PD-1 rechallenge after CTLA-4-related grade 3 colitis is feasible in selected patients with acceptable re-toxicity rates
- Published case series and retrospective data support rechallenge with anti-PD-1 after steroid management in patients who achieve complete resolution of colitis

Plan: Rechallenge with Opdivo monotherapy 480 mg IV every 4 weeks is planned after the following criteria are met:
  1. Complete clinical resolution of colitis (stool frequency ≤1-2 per day, no blood, no cramping)
  2. Complete or near-complete steroid taper (prednisone dose ≤10 mg/day)
  3. Endoscopic confirmation of mucosal healing strongly preferred (per gastroenterology)
  4. CRP normalized
  This decision will not be made for at minimum 8-12 weeks. I have communicated this timeline clearly to the patient. She understands and accepts the plan.

PROBLEM 4: ONCOLOGIC STATUS — MELANOMA

Incidentally, the CT obtained on admission shows ongoing partial response of the hepatic and nodal disease to Yervoy/nivo, despite only 3 cycles having been administered. This is a positive signal confirming immunotherapy activity in this case. LDH is trending down from its peak of 512 U/L at progression on BRAFi/MEKi to currently 312 U/L. Although we are managing a significant toxicity, the tumor biology appears to be responding favorably, which further supports the plan for Opdivo maintenance rechallenge after toxicity resolution.

No new anti-cancer therapy to be initiated during this acute hospitalization.

PROBLEM 5: HYPOTHYROIDISM / IMMUNE-RELATED ENDOCRINOPATHY MONITORING

Baseline hypothyroidism is present (pre-existing, on levothyroxine 75 mcg). TSH today 3.4 mIU/L on current dose. Free T4 low-normal. Checkpoint inhibitors — particularly anti-PD-1 agents — carry a well-known risk of immune-mediated thyroiditis and hypothyroidism, either worsening pre-existing disease or de novo. We will monitor thyroid function closely with repeat TSH and free T4 at discharge and at first outpatient follow-up. Endocrinology is available for comanagement if thyroid abnormalities worsen.

PROBLEM 6: ANEMIA / NUTRITIONAL STATUS

Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL — mild anemia in the context of GI blood loss and chronic inflammatory state. Iron studies to be obtained. If iron deficient, oral iron supplementation after GI inflammation resolves. No indication for transfusion at this time. Albumin mildly low (3.4 g/dL); dietitian consultation placed for nutritional support. Multivitamin added.

DISPOSITION

Medically improving. Anticipate transition to oral steroids tomorrow (hospital day 5) if stool frequency continues to improve. Plan for discharge on hospital day 5-6 once she is tolerating oral prednisone, oral intake is adequate, vital signs are stable, and she has received patient education on steroid taper schedule, signs of colitis relapse (bloody diarrhea, >4 BMs/day, cramping, fever), and warning signs requiring return to the emergency department.

DISCHARGE PLANNING / FOLLOW-UP

1. Oncology outpatient follow-up within 2 weeks of discharge to reassess clinical status and steroid taper compliance
2. Gastroenterology follow-up in 4-6 weeks for clinical reassessment; colonoscopy scheduling to assess mucosal healing before Opdivo rechallenge
3. Endocrinology referral placed for thyroid monitoring given ongoing anti-PD-1 therapy (pending)
4. Primary care physician to be updated regarding current hospitalization, steroid therapy, and implications for long-term management
5. Dietitian consultation before discharge

PATIENT EDUCATION

I spent approximately 30 minutes with Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo and her husband at bedside today reviewing:
- What happened: immune system over-activation caused by ipi/Opdivo attacking the colon lining — this is an expected but serious side effect of this class of medication
- Why ipi is being permanently stopped (non-negotiable; too dangerous to rechallenge)
- The reasoning for eventually rechallenging with nivo monotherapy and why this is in her interest
- Importance of not stopping prednisone abruptly (risk of adrenal insufficiency and colitis flare)
- When to return to ED: >4-5 stools/day, blood in stool, severe cramping, fever, or inability to take oral medications
- Encouraging news regarding tumor response seen on imaging

The patient verbalized understanding of the plan and expressed her desire to continue treatment when safe to do so. She appeared emotionally resilient and appropriately engaged. Her husband was supportive and asked thoughtful questions.

Overall, this patient is navigating a serious treatment complication but is on a favorable clinical trajectory. The underlying melanoma appears to be responding to immunotherapy, and I remain cautiously optimistic that controlled nivo rechallenge after complete toxicity resolution will provide durable disease control in this high-TMB, PD-L1-positive patient.

Cancer status: Present, with active disease — unable to formally assess treatment response in the context of acute immune toxicity management; however, incidental CT findings during this admission are consistent with ongoing response to prior immunotherapy.

---

Electronically signed,
[Attending Oncologist]
Hematology/Oncology
Time of consultation: [TIME]

CC: Gastroenterology, Hospital Medicine, Primary Oncologist
